name: master mint cilantro skillet
instructions:
THREE VARIANTS. All share the mint + cilantro herb pair and indian-spice register, but the structural patterns are different — variants A and B are mortar-pestle-or-chop herb-sauce-on-protein (priya krishna); variant C is a tarka-style dal (madhur jaffrey).

=== VARIANT A — CHICKEN (PRIYA KRISHNA) ===

INGREDIENTS:
- chicken breast (thigh works but breast is preferred — see notes)
- turmeric, chili powder, cumin, ground coriander
- ghee (canonical fat)
- salt

HERB FINISH INGREDIENTS:
- fresh cilantro
- fresh mint
- garlic
- ginger
- lime

PROCEDURE:
1. Cook chicken breast in ghee with turmeric, chili powder, cumin, and coriander until done.
2. Meanwhile, prepare the herb finish (see HERB FINISH TECHNIQUE below — mash/chop-in-pan is the default; mortar-pestle also works; chimichurri as a side option).
3. If using the mash-in-pan approach: chop or mash cilantro, mint, garlic, ginger, and lime, then add to the pan with the cooked chicken at the very end. Toss briefly off heat.
4. If using chimichurri: plate the chicken, serve mint-cilantro-chimichurri on bread or naan on the side.

=== VARIANT B — TOFU ===

INGREDIENTS:
- firm tofu, sliced AS THIN AS POSSIBLE (see notes)
- ghee (canonical fat — see notes on ghee-not-vegetable-oil)
- turmeric, chili powder, cumin, ground coriander
- salt
- (optional) starch (potato or corn) — only if pressing the tofu, for a crispy exterior

HERB FINISH INGREDIENTS:
- fresh cilantro
- fresh mint
- garlic
- ginger
- lime

PROCEDURE:
1. Slice the tofu as thin as you can (a knife or a sharp mandoline both work). Either PRESS (to remove water) or BOIL briefly (~5 min in salted water then pat dry). Both are acceptable prep paths.
2. If pressed: toss the slices in a light dusting of starch. This gives a crispy exterior when pan-cooked. Skip if you boiled.
3. Pan-cook the tofu slices in ghee with turmeric, chili powder, cumin, and coriander until golden on both sides. Use ghee generously — the ghee is doing the flavor work in this variant. Do NOT add vegetable oil (or minimize it if you must).
4. Herb finish: same as chicken variant — mash-in-pan is the default. Chimichurri on bread/naan on the side is also fine.

=== VARIANT C — LENTILS (MADHUR JAFFREY, TARKA-STYLE DAL) ===

INGREDIENTS:
- 1 tbsp vegetable oil (this variant is the exception — tarka wants neutral oil, not ghee)
- 1/2 tsp cumin seeds, 1/2 tsp mustard seeds, pinch of asafetida, 1-3 whole dried red chiles
- 1 small yellow onion, fine half rings
- 2 cloves garlic, chopped
- 1 medium tomato, chopped
- 1 cup whole green lentils
- 3/4 tsp salt, 1 tsp ground coriander
- 1/2 cup chopped fresh cilantro + 1/2 cup chopped fresh mint (added off heat at the end)

PROCEDURE:
1. Bloom whole spices in 1 tbsp vegetable oil: cumin seeds, mustard seeds, asafetida, and dried red chiles.
2. Add yellow onion half rings, sauté briefly. Add garlic, then chopped tomato.
3. Add lentils, water to cover, salt, and ground coriander. Simmer until lentils are tender.
4. Off heat, stir in the chopped fresh cilantro and mint. No mortar work; no chimichurri; the herbs go in raw at the end.
notes:
TWO STRUCTURAL PATTERNS UNDER ONE HERB PAIR:
- Chicken and tofu (variants A and B — priya krishna pattern): mortar-pestle-or-chop herb finish on a spice-cooked protein. The herb component goes on at the END, never cooked in.
- Lentils (variant C — madhur jaffrey pattern): tarka-style dal with whole spices bloomed in oil, onion/tomato base, herbs stirred in off-heat at the finish.
The herb pair (mint + cilantro) is the through-line; everything else is variant-specific.

DO NOT COOK THE HERBS IN — VARIANTS A AND B: this is the key technique across the chicken and tofu variants. Treating these like a mole and cooking the herb mash into a sauce makes the herbs lose flavor and turns the oil an unappetizing green. The herb component is applied at the end, off heat or just barely warm.

=== HERB FINISH TECHNIQUE — VARIANTS A AND B ===

MASH OR CHOP AND ADD TO THE PAN IS THE DEFAULT (LIKE A SAAG DISH): the best all-purpose herb finish for variants A and B is to just chop or mash the cilantro + mint + garlic + ginger + lime and add it to the pan at the very end (off heat). This is the same move a saag curry makes — coarsely chopped or lightly mashed fresh herbs folded in raw at the finish. Fast, no separate condiment to make, no serving-vessel considerations. This should be your first choice.

MORTAR-PESTLE MASH — PESTO-STYLE (MY PREFERRED REFINEMENT, NOT PRIYA'S ORIGINAL): pounding cilantro + mint + garlic + ginger + lime in a mortar produces a slightly more integrated paste with released oils and a smoother mouthfeel than chopping does. Treat it like a pesto — pounded into a rough paste, spooned on top at the end. Worth the mortar pull for that specific refinement; the chop-in-pan default gets you 90% of the way there with less work.

PRIYA KRISHNA'S ORIGINAL VERSION IS A BLENDER MARINADE — I DON'T LIKE IT AS MUCH: for the record: Priya Krishna's original technique doesn't mash the herbs like a pesto. She blends them smooth in a blender and uses the result as a marinade for the chicken before cooking. This works — it's the source recipe — but I don't prefer it. The blender-and-marinade approach cooks the herbs into the protein (some of the marinade is on the surface as it hits the pan), which fights the don't-cook-the-herbs rule that makes variants A and B taste bright and green. Marinated-then-cooked chicken loses the mint-and-cilantro-forward finish that the mash-at-the-end approach preserves. If you're a Priya loyalist go for the marinade; my strong recommendation is the mash-in-pan or mortar-pesto finish instead.

CHIMICHURRI AS A SIDE OPTION — OK BUT NOT PREFERRED, AND ONLY IF SERVED ON BREAD/NAAN: mint-cilantro-chimichurri (see mint-cilantro-chimichurri recipe) works alongside variants A or B, but ONLY as a side condiment served on bread or naan — not spooned directly onto the protein. The chimichurri is a raw allium + vinegar condiment; putting it directly on the pan-cooked chicken or tofu makes the plate read as two dishes (indian pan-cook + argentinian-shape condiment), whereas on bread it functions as a separate scoopable thing that doesn't compete with the primary flavor axis. Preferred order: mash-in-pan > mortar-pestle > chimichurri-on-bread. Skip chimichurri entirely if you don't have bread/naan on the side.

=== VARIANT A NOTES (CHICKEN) ===

CHICKEN BREAST OVER THIGH: priya krishna's recipe works better with chicken breast than thigh. Breast carries the spice-and-herb register cleanly; thigh's fattiness competes with the bright herb finish.

=== VARIANT B NOTES (TOFU) ===

SLICE THE TOFU AS THIN AS POSSIBLE: thin slices are what make this variant work. A knife or sharp mandoline both work — the goal is slices thin enough that the spices penetrate the tofu quickly and the surface-area-to-volume ratio favors flavor development. Thick slabs read as bland-tofu-with-spice-crust rather than fully-flavored tofu.

PRESS OR BOIL — BOTH ARE FINE: two acceptable water-removal paths.
- PRESS: 15-20 min under weight, patted dry. Denser texture. If pressed, you can toss the slices in a light dusting of starch (potato or corn) before pan-cooking to get a crispy exterior.
- BOIL: brief ~5 min boil in salted water, then pat dry. The surface protein sets and drives out water; slices hold together well in the pan. No starch coating with the boiled path.
Both work; the choice comes down to whether you want the crispy-starch-crust texture (press + starch) or the softer-inside texture (boil).

GHEE IS THE CANONICAL FAT — DON'T USE VEGETABLE OIL: flavor in this variant is built mostly from ghee. Use ghee generously; the browned tofu picks up nutty ghee character that vegetable oil can't provide. Do NOT add vegetable oil — or minimize it aggressively if you must (say, to boost the smoke point). This is unlike a lot of indian pan-cooking where vegetable oil + ghee is a fine blend; here the tofu-plus-ghee-alone is the point.

=== VARIANT C NOTES (LENTILS) ===

TOMATO AND ASAFETIDA ARE THE LENTIL-SPECIFIC INGREDIENTS: unlike the chicken and tofu variants, the lentil variant uses tomato (one medium, chopped, into the tarka), asafetida (a pinch with the whole spices), mustard seeds, dried red chiles, and yellow onion. These are absent from variants A and B — they belong to the dal pattern, not the mortar-pestle-herb-sauce pattern. Don't backport them to the chicken or tofu variants.

NO GINGER, LIME, TURMERIC, OR CHILI POWDER IN VARIANT C: the dal variant skips ginger and lime (the mortar-pestle brightness slot is handled differently here) and uses dried red chiles + asafetida instead of turmeric and chili powder. The two patterns have separate spice axes.

VEGETABLE OIL IN VARIANT C — NOT GHEE: variant C is the exception to the ghee rule. A tarka wants neutral vegetable oil to carry the bloomed whole spices; ghee's own character would compete with the mustard-seed-and-asafetida top note. 1 tbsp is enough (madhur jaffrey calls for 3; that's too much).

HERBS STIRRED IN AT THE END — NO MORTAR: variant C doesn't get the mortar-pestle treatment. The cilantro and mint are chopped and folded in raw at the finish, off heat. Same don't-cook-the-herbs principle as variants A and B, different application.

=== SHARED CROSS-VARIANT NOTES ===

MINT CILANTRO CHIMICHURRI PAIRING (A AND B ONLY): the chimichurri recipe (variant A — indian, with lime and ginger) is a natural condiment ONLY for the chicken and tofu variants here, and even then only on bread/naan on the side. Not appropriate for variant C — the tarka-style dal has its own herbal finish and the chimichurri's vinegar would fight the tomato and asafetida base.

KASHMIRI CHILI POWDER AND RED CHILI FLAKES ARE BOTH NECESSARY — SAME RULE AS PALAK PANEER (SEE PESTLE-SAAG-CURRY): the indian chile stack for this dish is generic chili powder + kashmiri chili powder + red chili flakes. Each does a different job:
- KASHMIRI CHILI POWDER: brings bright red color and gentle warming heat without aggression. This is what makes the finished dish visually indian rather than reading as generic pan-cooked-meat-with-herbs. Non-optional.
- RED CHILI FLAKES: the whole-form chile presence. In variant C the flakes represent the whole dried red chiles bloomed in the tarka. In variants A and B they can be scattered on the pan-cook for a heat-and-visual accent.
- GENERIC CHILI POWDER: covers the broad heat + earthy character.
Same rule holds in the palak paneer branches (see master-indian-saag-curry) — the indian chile stack there wants the same kashmiri + flakes + generic combination for the same reasons.
